thinking.text:
Let me think about this differently. The current approach of capturing unique commands from all frames is capturing partial commands as they're being typed. The game has 262 moves according to the final score. I need a different strategy.

Let me examine the OCR outputs more carefully. Each frame shows a portion of the game transcript. Commands appear after '>' prompts. The transcript scrolls, so commands at the top of the view will eventually scroll off as new commands are added at the bottom.

The key insight is: I should track commands as they appear in the transcript area (not the typing line) and detect when a NEW command appears that wasn't there before. This indicates a command was just executed.

But a simpler approach: I can look at the last few frames which should show the entire game history up to that point, and if I process frames in order, I can build up the command list by tracking what's new in each frame.

Actually, let me look at this from a different angle. The game summary says 262 moves. I have 190 frames at 1fps. Each frame shows maybe 5-10 commands in the visible transcript. So across all frames, I should see most of the 262 commands.

The problem is that my current approach captures partial commands (the one being typed) as well as historical commands. I should focus on the historical commands only (all `>` lines except the last one in each frame).

Let me write a script that:
1. For each frame, extracts all commands (lines after `>`)
2. Removes the last one (the one being typed)
3. Tracks all historical commands seen, in order of first appearance
